title: Combat Lifesaver Extended Care
summary: Extended field care protocols for when evacuation is delayed 6 or more hours — reassessment cycles, pain management, wound care, hydration and nutrition, vital sign monitoring without equipment, and casualty packaging for eventual evacuation.

steps.title: Reassessment Cycle
steps.body: Extended care begins where initial trauma care ends. Every 15 minutes, reassess in this order: (1) Airway — verify position is maintained (unconscious casualty may need repositioning if recovery position was used); suction or clear secretions if available; (2) Breathing — count respiratory rate (normal: 12–20 breaths per minute); note quality (shallow, labored, gurgling indicate deterioration); (3) Circulation — check tourniquet: if more than 2 hours have passed since application, attempt conversion to wound packing with direct pressure if bleeding has stopped and evacuation is not imminent; document tourniquet application time prominently (on forehead with marker, or on tourniquet tag); (4) Consciousness level using AVPU scale: Alert (awake, oriented), Voice (responds to voice), Pain (responds only to painful stimulus), Unresponsive; any downward change is significant; (5) Temperature — check for hypothermia continuously, not once; wet or shaded casualty loses core temperature rapidly; add insulation, remove wet clothing, use vapor barrier. Log each assessment with time.

steps.title: Pain Management in the Field
steps.body: Untreated severe pain causes physiological stress response that worsens outcomes — pain management is a medical intervention, not a comfort measure. Available analgesics ranked by preference: (1) Meloxicam 15mg orally — NSAID with anti-inflammatory properties; preferred for musculoskeletal injuries, sprains, fractures without hemorrhage; safe for most casualties; give with food if available; (2) Acetaminophen 1,000mg orally — safe for all injury types including penetrating trauma; does not affect bleeding or hide internal hemorrhage signs; maximum 4g/24 hours total; (3) Ibuprofen 800mg orally — NSAID, effective but more GI side effects than meloxicam; adequate alternative if meloxicam unavailable. Critical contraindications: do NOT administer aspirin to any trauma casualty (antiplatelet effect increases bleeding); do NOT administer opioids or NSAIDs to penetrating torso trauma (opioids mask deterioration signs indicating internal bleeding; NSAIDs impair clotting). Opioid administration requires specific training, authorization, and ongoing monitoring capability.

steps.title: Wound Care — Extended Protocols
steps.body: Initial hemostasis (stopping bleeding) must transition to infection prevention in extended care. Dressing changes: change wound dressings every 24 hours minimum, or immediately when saturated; note wound appearance at each change. Wound irrigation: clean wounds with irrigation before dressing change; improvised irrigation device: fill 20cc syringe, attach 18-gauge angiocath or improvised nozzle, irrigate at 8 PSI (arm's length from wound); clean water is acceptable when sterile saline is unavailable; irrigate until visibly clear. Wound closure decisions: primary closure (suturing or closure strips) is appropriate ONLY for clean wounds that are less than 6 hours old with no contamination and no signs of infection; contaminated wounds, bite wounds, or combat wounds should be left open; delayed primary closure (3–5 days post-injury) is preferred for contaminated wounds that are clean and uninfected at reassessment. Infection signs requiring urgent intervention: increasing redness with advancing border, localized warmth and swelling, purulent discharge, fever above 101°F, or red streaking extending from wound (lymphangitis = systemic infection, urgent).

steps.title: Hydration and Nutrition in Extended Care
steps.body: Fluid and nutritional support become critical when evacuation extends beyond 6–12 hours. Conscious casualty oral hydration: offer small, frequent sips rather than large amounts at once; minimum 2 liters per day for a wounded adult, more in heat or with significant wound drainage; oral hydration takes priority over IV in a conscious, non-vomiting casualty with intact gut. Monitoring hydration status: urine color is the most accessible indicator — dark yellow to amber = dehydrated, pale yellow = adequate, colorless = over-hydrated (rare in field); absence of urination for 6+ hours = significant dehydration or shock. Nutrition: conscious casualties with intact gut and non-abdominal injuries can begin small amounts of food after 24 hours — high protein is preferred for wound healing; do not attempt to feed unconscious casualties. IV/IO fluid management (if trained and equipped): Lactated Ringer's is preferred over normal saline for large-volume resuscitation (lower risk of hyperchloremic acidosis); if hemorrhage is not fully controlled, limit IV fluids to 500mL–1L and target a palpable radial pulse (permissive hypotension) rather than normal blood pressure — aggressive IV fluid resuscitation before hemorrhage control 'pops the clot.'

steps.title: Monitoring Vital Signs Without Equipment
steps.body: Manual vital sign assessment provides essential information about casualty trajectory. Heart rate: locate radial pulse (wrist, thumb side); if not palpable, check carotid pulse in neck; count beats for 30 seconds and multiply by 2; normal: 60–100 bpm; trauma patients often run 100–120; very fast (>120) plus weak, thready pulse plus pale, cool, clammy skin = shock, reassess hemorrhage control immediately. Respiratory rate: count chest rises for 30 seconds and multiply by 2; normal: 12–20; above 30 or below 8 = critical — reassess airway and breathing interventions. Temperature (without thermometer): assess forehead warmth (back of hand most sensitive); shivering and goosebumps = hypothermia developing; flushed, dry skin plus hot forehead = fever or heat illness. Mental status (AVPU + orientation): ask three questions — 'What is your name? Where are you right now? What year is it?' Confusion or inability to answer indicates hypoxia, hemorrhagic shock, head injury, hypoglycemia, or sepsis; note baseline at first assessment and track any changes; deterioration in mental status is the most sensitive early indicator of worsening condition.

steps.title: Casualty Packaging for Evacuation
steps.body: When evacuation becomes possible, proper documentation and physical preparation determine continuity of care. Documentation — the MIST report: Mechanism of injury (fall, gunshot, blunt trauma, burn); Injuries found (anatomic description — 'gunshot wound right thigh, tourniquet applied, wound packed'); Signs and symptoms at time of handoff (last vital signs, mental status); Treatment given (all interventions with times: tourniquet applied 14:35, morphine 5mg IV 15:10, 500mL LR infusing). Record medications given with dose, route, and time — the receiving provider must know. Allergy documentation must accompany the casualty. Physical packaging: wrap casualty in insulating layer (blankets, sleeping bag, poncho liner) to prevent further heat loss during transport; secure casualty to litter or improvised stretcher; spinal precautions (manual stabilization and padding, not necessarily a rigid collar) for: falls greater than 3 times the patient's height, vehicle accidents with intrusion, diving injuries, or any unconscious trauma patient with unknown mechanism. Continuously reassess airway during transport — position changes can compromise an airway that was patent at rest.

warnings: Extended trauma care requires hands-on training — reading alone is insufficient preparation. Take a Wilderness First Responder (WFR) or Combat Lifesaver (CLS) course before a grid-down scenario.

Tourniquet conversion (removing a tourniquet after extended application) carries risk of rebleeding and reperfusion injury — only attempt when you are confident in your wound packing skills and can manage potential rebleeding.
proTips: Write the tourniquet application time on the patient's forehead with a marker — 'TK 14:35' — so it is visible to anyone who encounters the casualty, even if your written notes are lost.
The MIST report takes 60 seconds to recite and dramatically improves continuity of care at handoff. Practice saying it aloud during training so it becomes automatic under stress.
whenNotTo: Do not attempt tourniquet conversion (removing an applied tourniquet) without confidence in wound packing skills and ability to manage rebleeding — rebleeding from premature conversion can be fatal
Do not administer opioids or NSAIDs to casualties with penetrating torso trauma — opioids mask deterioration signs; NSAIDs impair clotting
Do not attempt primary wound closure (suturing or closure strips) on contaminated wounds, bite wounds, or combat wounds — leave open for delayed primary closure at 3-5 days

quickGlance.situation: A casualty has received initial trauma care but evacuation is delayed 6 or more hours — extended care protocols apply
immediate: Reassess every 15 minutes in order: Airway → Breathing (count RR) → Circulation (tourniquet check, pulse quality) → Consciousness (AVPU) → Temperature
Pain management: Meloxicam 15mg or Acetaminophen 1000mg orally — do NOT give aspirin or NSAIDs to penetrating torso trauma
Document tourniquet application time prominently on forehead with marker: 'TK 14:35'
then: Oral hydration for conscious casualty: minimum 2 liters per day — monitor urine color (pale yellow = adequate)
Prepare MIST report for handoff: Mechanism, Injuries, Signs/symptoms, Treatment given with times
